verses.commentary: Angels of God met him.
God's camp and Jacob's camp. Double protection.
Preparing for reunion after twenty years.
Message to Esau addresses him as 'my lord,' Jacob as 'your servant.' Extreme submissive language.
Sending message. To find favor.
Esau coming. Army-sized force.
In great fear and distress Jacob divided the people who were with him into two groups, and the flocks and herds and camels as well.
Survival planning.
Appealing to God's command and promise.
I am unworthy of all the kindness (chesed, חֶסֶד) and faithfulness. Humble recognition: I crossed Jordan with only a staff, now I have two camps.
Attack me and mothers with children. Honest fear.
Your promise: descendants countless as sand. Jacob reminds God of his covenant promises. Faith appeals to divine faithfulness.
Spent the night, then selected gifts from his possessions. Strategic preparation.
Detailed gift list follows: hundreds of animals in waves. Massive bribe.
More animals. Lavish gift.
Space between herds. Waves of gifts.
The questions force Esau to think before reacting. Strategic ambiguity.
When Esau asks whose animals these are, say: your servant Jacob's. Submissive formula repeated.
Each wave reinforces Jacob's submission and generosity.
Jacob's thinking: I will pacify (literally cover his face) with gifts.
Gifts go ahead as buffer. Jacob waits behind them.
That night. Crossed Jabbok.
Jacob sends his family across, preparing for what comes.
Jacob left alone. The wrestling continues till daybreak. Hosea 12:3-4 identifies the opponent as God/angel.
Jacob's strength disabled but he doesn't give up.
Let me go, for it is daybreak. But Jacob holds on: I will not let you go unless you bless me.
The name question forces identity confession. Jacob must admit who he is.
You have struggled (sarah, שָׂרָה) with God and humans and have overcome. New identity: Israel means one who struggles with God.
The blessing is given but the name withheld. Mystery remains. Divine names aren't casually given. Blessing comes first, full revelation comes later.
My life spared. You cannot see God and live (Exodus 33:20), yet Jacob survives.
The man who once ran (27:43) now limps.
Dietary memorial. Physical reminder of spiritual encounter.
